title: Pic 5
caption: Pic 5: The Palace of Culture and Science, built after World War II, stands near the former Warsaw Ghetto and symbolizes the city's reconstruction following the devastation of the Holocaust.

title: Pic 6
caption: Pic 6: “Ulica Raoula Wallenberga” means “Raoul Wallenberg Street.” It commemorates Raoul Wallenberg, the Swedish diplomat who saved tens of thousands of Hungarian Jews during the Holocaust by issuing protective passports and establishing safe houses in Budapest.

title: Pic 13
caption: Pic 13: The Monument to the Ghetto Heroes commemorates the Jewish fighters and civilians of the Warsaw Ghetto and honors those who resisted Nazi persecution.

title: Pic 14
caption: Pic 14: Built after World War II, the Palace of Culture and Science overlooks areas once devastated during the Holocaust and symbolizes Warsaw's postwar reconstruction.

title: Pic 15
caption: Pic 15: Warsaw's modern skyline reflects the city's rebuilding after the destruction brought by World War II and the Holocaust.

title: Pic 16
caption: Pic 16: This monument honors Janusz Korczak, the educator who chose to remain with the children of his orphanage and accompanied them from the Warsaw Ghetto to their deaths during the Holocaust.

title: Pic 17
caption: Pic 17: Another look at the Monument to the Ghetto Heroes in Warsaw honors the Jewish fighters and victims of the Warsaw Ghetto and commemorates the 1943 Warsaw Ghetto Uprising.

title: Pic 18
caption: Pic 18: These steel structures mark the location of the wooden footbridge that connected the two sections of the Warsaw Ghetto, allowing victims to cross above Chłodna Street while remaining confined within the ghetto.

title: Pic 19
caption: Pic 19: The night sky above the former Warsaw Ghetto serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving the memory of the Jewish community that once flourished in Warsaw.

title: Pic 20
caption: Pic 20: The Westin Warsaw hotel stands in the heart of a city rebuilt after the devastation of World War II and the Holocaust, reflecting Warsaw's transformation into a modern European capital.

title: Pic 1

title: Pic 3

title: Pic 4
caption: Pic 4: Camp clothing belonging to Helena Kurcyusz, including shoes, a striped jacket, and a headscarf

title: Pic 5
caption: Pic 5: Close-up view of the camp clothing belonging to Helena Kurcyusz

title: Pic 6
caption: Pic 6: Photographs recovered from the grounds of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 8
caption: Pic 8: Biography of Jerzy Pfeffer, a survivor of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 9
caption: Pic 9: Drawing of a carpenter created by Helena Kurcyusz

title: Pic 10
caption: Pic 10: Cross given to Bronisława Korzeniowska (née Milewska) by Amour Collin

title: Pic 11
caption: Pic 11: Shoes confiscated from prisoners upon their arrival at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 12
caption: Pic 12: Overcrowded and unhygienic bunk beds in the prisoners’ barracks at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 13
caption: Pic 13: Memorial exhibit at the Majdanek Concentration Camp museum featuring a fragment of a camp road constructed from stones taken from Jewish tombstones (matzevot)

title: Pic 14
caption: Pic 14: Watchtower at the Majdanek Concentration Camp used by Nazi officials to observe inmates

title: Pic 15
caption: Pic 15: The Column of the Three Eagles, the first monument to the victims of the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 16
caption: Pic 16: Description of the Column of the Three Eagles monument at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 17
caption: Pic 17: Side view of the camp grounds at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 18
caption: Pic 18: The Mausoleum at the Majdanek Concentration Camp, inscribed with “Los nasz dla was przestrogą” (“Our fate is a warning to you”)

title: Pic 19
caption: Pic 19: Side view of the Mausoleum

title: Pic 20
caption: Pic 20: Crematorium at the Majdanek Concentration Camp

title: Pic 21
caption: Pic 21: This memorial stone at the Majdanek Concentration Camp in Lublin commemorates the approximately 18,400 Jewish prisoners murdered on November 3, 1943, during Operation Harvest Festival (Erntefest), whose bodies were buried in trenches nearby, and serves today as a site of remembrance for the victims of the Holocaust.
Pic 1: View from the outside of Belzec, one of many Nazi death camps where Jews were sent to be exterminated
Pic 2: Path that Jews would walk on as they approached the gas chambers in Belzec
Pic 3: View from the end of the path that Jews would walk on as they approached the gas chambers in Belzec
Pic 4: Memorial to the Jews lost in Belzec, as well as a call to action
Pic 5: The rubble of Belzec, which was left there so that the camp’s existence and the atrocities committed couldn’t be erased and forgotten
Pic 6: Railroad tracks stacked on top of each other that were part of the railroad that brought Jews to Belzec
Pic 7: View of shoes within the Belzec museum that were taken from the Jews processed and killed there
Pic 8: View of moat surrounding Zamość Fortress
Pic 9: View down a tunnel connected Zamość Fortress
Pic 10: View down a street of Zamość
Pic 11: View down another street of Zamość
Pic 12: Torah scroll left behind by traveling Jews during the Nazis advance that was eventually donated to a synagogue (might need to correct me I cant remember exactly)
Pic 13: Memorial of Allan Gerson, son of Holocaust survivors and attorney.
Pic 14: View of Torah scroll within a synagogue in Zamość
Pic 15: Buildings in the Great Market Square in Zamość
Pic 16: Zamość Town Hall
Pic 17: View down a street in the Great Market Square in Zamość
Pic 18: (I don’t know the context of this statue) next to the Parish of the Resurrection of the Lord and St. Thomas the Apostle in Zamość
Pic 19: Statue of St. John Paul II, who was pope from 1978 to 2005, outside of the Parish of the Resurrection of the Lord and St. Thomas the Apostle in Zamość
Pic 20: View of the sunset near the Zamość Fortress
